raw.regions_all: İfrîkıye; Mısır; Güney Suriye; Filistin; Sicilya; Hicaz; Yemen (davet)
raw.narrative_en: The Fatimids were a dynasty founded on the Ismaili Shia caliphal claim that created Cairo as its capital, building an alternative Islamic center to the Abbasids. They founded al-Azhar, leaving one of the Islamic world's most enduring educational institutions; Druzism took shape under al-Hakim, while the Nizari-Musta'li split determined the future of the Ismaili movement.

raw.notes: Founded by Idrisid-era settlers of Fes; Fatima al-Fihri founded Al Quaraouiyine
Pasha of Marrakech dynasty; Thami El Glaoui

raw.narrative_en: The Almohads rose through a radical religious reform movement based on Ibn Tumart's doctrine of divine unity, building the largest empire of the Islamic West from the Maghreb to the Iberian Peninsula. While patronizing philosophers like Ibn Rushd and Ibn Tufayl, their defeat at Las Navas de Tolosa (1212) initiated the empire's dissolution.

raw.regions_all: Fas; Batı/Orta Cezayir; Endülüs; Sahra; Moritanya
raw.narrative_en: The Almoravids emerged from the veiled Sanhaja warriors of the Sahara, shaped by ribat discipline and spreading Maliki orthodoxy by the sword. Their victory at Zallaqa rescued al-Andalus from Christian advance while building a vast empire spanning the Maghreb to the Iberian Peninsula.

raw.capital: Kayrevân; al-Mahdiyye (Zîrîler); Qal'at Banī Hammād; Bicâye/Bougie (Hammâdîler)
raw.narrative_en: The Zirids and Hammadids were Sanhaja Berber dynasties that transitioned from Fatimid service to independence. Zirid emir al-Mu'izz's declaration of Abbasid allegiance provoked the Fatimids to redirect the Hilalian and Sulaym Bedouin tribes toward the Maghreb, a migration wave that permanently altered North Africa's demographic structure.

raw.regions_all: İfrîkıye; Cezayir; Sicilya; Malta; Güney İtalya kıyıları
raw.narrative_en: The Aghlabids transformed their Ifriqiyan governorship from the Abbasid caliph into de facto independence, conquering Sicily and carrying Islamic culture into Europe. They developed Kairouan as a center of learning and commerce while projecting naval power across the Mediterranean with raids on Italian coasts.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The 'Abd al-Wādids or Zayyānids or Ziyānids
raw.narrative_en: The Abd al-Wadids were a Zanata Berber dynasty that established an independent principality centered on Tlemcen after the Almohad collapse. They prospered through Tlemcen's strategic position on east-west trade routes but remained perpetually squeezed between Marinid and Hafsid pressures, never achieving great imperial stature.

The Hafsids began as Almohad governors before gaining independence, becoming the most important dynasty of medieval Ifriqiya. They gained legitimacy through caliphal claims while revitalizing the region's intellectual tradition by introducing the madrasa system to Ifriqiya.

raw.regions_all: Batı Cezayir; Aurés; Güney Tunus; Cebelinefûsa bağlantıları
raw.narrative_en: The Rustamids were an Iranian-origin dynasty established as the political center of the Kharijite Ibadi school, building the cosmopolitan city of Tahert, nicknamed the 'Iraq of the Maghreb.' They prospered through their connection to trans-Saharan trade networks while maintaining sectarian tolerance that sustained diverse communities.

raw.region_primary: Anadolu → Güneydoğu Avrupa → Ortadoğu → Kuzey Afrika
raw.regions_all: Söğüt/Bursa (erken); İstanbul; Anadolu; Balkanlar; Suriye; Mısır; Hicaz; Irak; Kuzey Afrika; Kırım
raw.narrative_en: The Ottomans established the longest-lasting and largest empire in Islamic history, spanning three continents over six centuries. Rising from a small Anatolian frontier principality to world power, the Ottomans became heirs to the Roman Empire through the conquest of Constantinople (1453) and the political and religious leader of the Islamic world by assuming the caliphate. Reaching the zenith of law, architecture, and military power under Süleyman the Magnificent, the empire created a distinctive civilizational model through Sinan's masterpieces, the kapıkulu system, and the multi-religious millet structure. This dynasty of 36 sultans exited the historical stage after World War I defeat through the National Struggle and the proclamation of the Republic.

raw.regions_all: Balear Adaları; Doğu Mağrib (İfrîkıye bölgesinde faaliyetler)
raw.narrative_en: The Banu Ghaniya were the last remnant of the Almoravid dynasty in the Balearic Islands, maintaining independence as a resilient maritime principality for eighty years in the Mediterranean. They waged prolonged guerrilla warfare against the Almohads in eastern Maghreb, defending the declining Almoravid cause.

The Kalbids governed Sicily as governors loyal to the Fatimid caliph, transforming the island into the most advanced outpost of Islamic culture in Europe. While internal fragmentation paved the way for the Norman conquest, the Kalbid era laid the foundations of Sicily's multicultural identity.

raw.successor: Osmanlı doğrudan yönetiminin yeniden tesisi (Mahmud II)
raw.narrative_en: The Qaramanlis were a kul-origin dynasty that seized power in Ottoman Tripolitania, enriching themselves through Barbary piracy and ruling the region with de facto independence. They were liquidated by Ottoman Sultan Mahmud II's military intervention.

The Sanusi movement represents a rare transformation from a Sufi order founded by an Algerian-born sheikh into a modern state. Their resistance against Italian occupation formed the basis of Libyan national identity; King Idris gained independence but was overthrown in the 1969 coup.

The Alawite Sharifs have ruled Morocco from the 17th century to the present, one of the world's oldest reigning dynasties, descended from Prophet Muhammad. Moulay Ismail established a powerful centralized state, while the dynasty continued to defend Moroccan independence against European colonialism.

raw.notes: Current ruling house of Morocco; Sharifian lineage from Hassan ibn Ali

raw.regions_all: Fas; Kuzey Batı Mağrib; Rif; Volubilis (Walīla)
raw.successor: Fâtımî hâkimiyeti; ardından Hammûdîler (İspanya'da uzak kol)
raw.narrative_en: The Idrisids were founded by Idris, an Ahl al-Bayt descendant fleeing Abbasid persecution, who established in North Africa and transformed Fez into the first major cultural center at the western edge of the Islamic world. They initiated the systematic spread of Islam in the Maghreb and laid the foundations of the region's sharifian tradition.

raw.notes: First Moroccan Muslim dynasty; Sharifian; many living descendants

raw.narrative_en: The Midrarids were a Berber dynasty that controlled Sijilmasa, the key junction of trans-Saharan gold trade, serving as gatekeepers of the economic link between West Africa and the Mediterranean world. Their transition from Sufri Kharijism to Maliki Sunnism reflected an early manifestation of the Maghreb's sectarian transformation.

The Wattasids began as Marinid regents before becoming sultans in their own right, a Zanata Berber dynasty. They struggled against Portuguese coastal occupations and Spanish pressure while failing to contain the rise of the Sa'dian Sharifs from the south.

raw.notes: Berber dynasty of Morocco; successor to Marinids

raw.regions_all: Fas; Sûs; Nijer vadisi; Batı Afrika (Sudan)
raw.narrative_en: The Sa'dian Sharifs rose under the banner of jihad against Portuguese occupation, becoming the first dynasty to rule Morocco through sharifian legitimacy. Ahmad al-Mansur's Sudan expedition destroyed the Songhai Empire and brought trans-Saharan gold trade under direct control; his epithet 'the Golden' derived from this wealth.

The Husaynid Beys governed Tunisia for 250 years under Ottoman suzerainty, establishing a semi-independent administrative tradition. In the 19th century, their constitutional reform efforts presented one of the earliest modernization examples in the Islamic world.

raw.regions_all: Fas; kuzey Mağrib; kısmen Tilimsân; geçici olarak Tunus
raw.narrative_en: The Marinids were a Zanata Berber dynasty that ruled Morocco for two and a half centuries after the Almohad collapse. Unlike their predecessors, they lacked a strong religious ideology but supported scholarly traditions by building madrasas in Fez. Their efforts to protect Granada and intervene in the Iberian Peninsula remained limited.
